speaker: interviewee
text: We're having a yizkor this Sunday, so I'm going to give all the survivors one of your papers, if they want to make--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three, Mr. Harry Magid. Why did you have worms in your stomach?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to eat-- we couldn't cook anything, or we didn't have no hot water. We used to eat raw potatoes. We used to eat raw-- I mean, apples. They used to be green, but it wasn't the season. And I used to have live worms in my stomach. And we were-- we had thousands of thousands of lice, because we didn't-- for a year, we didn't-- we didn't have enough water to drink. We didn't wash ourself. And all the clothes that we had on rotted away on us. We was wearing rags. I mean, we didn't have nothing. Everything fell apart from-- from the sweat and from the-- from wearing one thing for a year and not to change. So. And it was unbelievable. I don't know how I could survive. And after I got liberated-- we got liberated, we were all bones. Couldn't even walk, because we didn't walk when we were laying in the ground or just going out to live. Because in the wintertime, it used to be very cold, and we did not have no clothes. And so you could freeze to death. And in the summertime, we were afraid to go out during the day because somebody'll see us, and they'll let know the police or the Germans. They'll come to kill us. And there was even some people that we knew that's hiding some Jewish people, and they-- they couldn't take it, because they killed all the family, everything. They committed suicide. And those two girls, what were-- well, they caught us in the forest, what four with us-- they survived. One ran away with a-- with a bullet in her back. From the-- from the grave, but they shot her. She lives now in New York. And the girl that I was on the wagon with her mother was shot in the arm and was bleeding. She survived, too. She lives in Australia. In Sydney, Australia. I receive letter from both of them. And I visited the girl in New York a lot of times, and I receive from her-- from both of them, almost every month, a letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a young boy, did you think you were going to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: We hoped. We didn't know we were going to survive. My father wanted to survive so badly. He wanted to-- to know what's gonna happen the end of the war with the-- with the Germans and those Ukrainian murderers, where they killed all those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at that time, did you have any clue as to how much longer you were going to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't. We were guessing. We were guessing. Maybe another day. Another day. Another-- we couldn't believe it, it will take so long. It took 19 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother was much younger. Was-- how did he survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my brother. We had a lot of trouble with my brother. He was seven years old, and he was hungry. He wanted food and used to cry. My father used to take his hand and hold his mouth, because we were afraid somebody was going to walk by and hear him crying, so they'll know that the Jews are hiding. So he used to hold him so tight, the mouth, that he used to get green.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he-- he did survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my brother survived. Yes. My brother survived, I survived, and my mother. And my mother is-- when she was 80 years old, she felt very guilty. Why did she have to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: So she tried to commit suicide. She said, why did they have to kill everybody, and she had to survive? And she is now in the Hancock Convalescent Hospital. The doctor she said she's got heart failure. She-- maybe she's got a month to live. When she was 80 was the first time that she wondered why she had survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. She got a-- a nervous breakdown, and she tried to commit suicide. She took a bottle-- a full bottle aspirin. And if she wouldn't vomit it out, she would be die. She would died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Have you ever been back to Stepan? No No. I never went-- right up to the war, I'd been there maybe about six months. But after that, I never wanted to go back. I receive a lot of letters from people from over there, and they're asking me to come to see. What is there to see? I'm going to go see the graves where they shot all the Jews? I have nothing to see there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's only one Jewish girl that I know that she lives in [NON-ENGLISH], about 30 kilometer from Stepan, and she made the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she made the matzevah for-- for when they shot the 3,000 Stepan Jews and plus in another place, they-- they shot-- about a mile from Stepan, on the [INAUDIBLE], they shot 538 Jews. So she made a big marble marker, and it says that here lays 3,000 Jews from Stepan.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war, were you completely surprised with vast numbers of Jews that had been killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew that they killed all the Jews. We knew, I mean, that-- because the few survivors that came back, they came looking for relatives. So it was no surprise to us. I mean, we were right there when they were shooting all the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yet you were not aware of the camps, the extermination camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We did not have camps in the Ukraine. The only camp they had, the concentration camp, was in Poland. It's only-- like Warsaw Lodge, all Krakow, all those Polish city. They-- they send them to the concentration camp. As they took out all the people, the Jews to the forest, must prepare ditches, and they shot them, and they threw them in the-- in the ditches.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- after liberation, when you moved from country to country, how did you-- how did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked, or we went by train. Because it was organized by the Jewish-- [? rihad ?] was [? ordered ?] from [INAUDIBLE] of [? hamad ?] organization. They were taking the Jews out of Poland and sending them to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother and your brother and you, did you all stay together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we all stayed together. We were in the-- for three years, in the [INAUDIBLE] camp in Wolyn, and then after that, we left to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is your brother now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother lives in Los Angeles. But he don't remember anything. He's-- hes-- everything blocked out. He don't remember nothing. He don't remember from Stepan or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: He doesn't remember being hungry and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was 11 at liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, seven. He was seven, I was 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: But after the liberation, how old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he doesn't remember nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your children? What have you told them over the years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told them plenty. But they just can't believe it. I mean, only my youngest son is interested in the liberation and all that-- what we went through. And the other ones, or they believe it, or they don't believe it. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about being Jewish? Did you maintain being Jewish all these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we have-- we have a kosher home, and my oldest son is a shomer Shabbos. He never works on Saturday or picks up a telephone or drives a car. And he-- he is a lawyer. He works for a very big firm in Los Angeles. And my daughter works for Cartier Jewelers in Beverly Hills. She works there over eight years. And my youngest son, Ben, is going to Northridge College. He's 23 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what about being Jewish? Did you have-- was that an issue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they all went-- they all went to yeshivas. I sent them all-- I wanted them to know that they are Jewish. I mean, they-- all went to [INAUDIBLE]. My oldest son, my daughter, and my youngest son, but the only one that sticks to Jewish is my oldest son. The other ones are different.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your faith in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I believe in God, but I can't forgive God but what they did to-- to so many innocent people. Such-- big rabbis and such. I mean, there was-- who knows what kind of people-- what-- what they would bring up in medicine. What they killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody knows what-- what they could have done, those people they killed. I mean, for nothing. I mean, for being only Jewish. To kill people? I mean, to destroy a nations?

speaker: interviewee
text: And nobody wanted to help. Not Roosevelt, not Churchill. Nobody wanted to help. They knew what was going on. They did not bomb the concentration camp. They did not bomb the railroads. They just-- if you were killing Jews, it was all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't know that at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't know. But after-- after the war, we knew that. The only Jews that mostly survived were they went to Russia. And from the-- from 6 million Jews, 3 and 1/2 million Jews died in Poland. And from-- in-- in Wolyn, what I come from, was 600,000 Jews. We survived only less than 1%.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have contact with these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have contact with all-- most all the survivors that survived from Stepan. A lot of them live in Israel. When I go to Israel, I visit them. That's all we talking for two, three days and nights, about Stepan. What we went through and about all the things that-- that we did in Stepan, you know? I mean, we don't sleep. We just talk and talk and talk and talk. And until we get sick of-- of all the-- what remind-- be reminding. And I get nightmares. A lot of times, I go through the whole thing, the killing and everything. And what-- what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you think something like this could happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] It can, if people are not going to know what went on. Nobody believed in Poland that they gonna-- gonna to kill so many innocent people. I mean, history can repeat itself. But even now in Poland, there's not-- there's maybe in all Poland, 5,000 Jews, and they're blaming everything on the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you think we can do here to prevent-- do you think it will ever happen in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to educate the people. Because if you're not going to educate them, people are ignorant. I mean, they-- if somebody tells them to-- to do something, they'll do it, even in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you see that happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: It can happen. We seen it coming in 1939, when they let out all the criminals from the jails. The first thing they did is they went to-- to kill Jews and take everything away with pipes, with sticks, with-- they were killing, and nobody said anything. You kill a Jew, that's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'll tell you what we'll do. We're going to take a look at your pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you can go through them and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'll explain what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father's picture. It was taken before he got married, in 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Joseph Magid. And he was killed by the Ukrainian, the end of 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do you think this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stepan. It was before-- before he got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture the couple months after we got liberated. This was taken in Stepan after the liberation, in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is from Odrej Kontratschuk and his wife. Those are the people that saved our life. They did everything for us. They were the most wonderful people in the world. That the way we felt about them. And we-- after we got liberated, we tried to help them all we could. We gave them, after we left Stepan, we gave them all our stuff that we had, and then we-- then we left. And this picture was taken in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wolosza. That was a village about 15 kilometer from Stepan. That was their-- their farm and their house. They had the one horse, that one cow, there were five sheeps, and there were a couple of pigs. That's all what they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the man's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Odrej Kontratschuk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the house you were hiding in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's near-- you cannot see the-- I-- I don't think you can see the barn or anything. That's-- we were hiding in their barn, not in their house.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother. It was taken in 1946. It was a reunion in Germany from the survivor from Stepan. And here is Samuel [? Baseman ?] on the right from my mother, and and those-- this is Samuel [? Baseman. ?] He lives here in Los Angeles. And this is-- I think, [? Yitzhak ?] [? Bornstein ?] and [? Maya ?] [? Goese. ?] And some of them, I don't even remember the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people had lived in this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stepan, we had 3,000 Jews, and we survived only 36. And this is Sonja Wiener, she lives now in Australia. This one, right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's still alive. I get letters from her. That was the woman who was with me on the wagon that the mother was shot in the arm. And she survived. She's got, I think, two sons and a daughter, but they live in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is her sister in this picture? Is her sister--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. She's only-- she didn't have a sister. She's the only one that-- she was the only child in that family. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And then those people are mostly all from around Stepan or from Stepan. And that was me in 1958, when I was getting married to Eva Long in Detroit, Michigan. That's our wedding-- first wedding picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was take-- this is my picture. It was taken on Benjamin's bar mitzvah, about 10 years ago. Now, on the bottom, in the middle, is my wife. On the right side is my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say everybody's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Vera. And on my left-- on my wife's left side, this is my wife, Eva. This is my mother, Frida. This is my wife's mother, Ellen. This is her husband. This is Ellen's husband. This is Zima, my brother's wife. This is Eliza, her daughter. This is Debbie, my brother's daughter. And this is my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alex. This is me again. This is my older son, Joseph. This is my uncle Pinchus. He lives in New York. This is my daughter, Vera. This is me again. Me with Benjamin. And this was my bar mitzvah boy, Benjamin. My wife, Eva. On the left side is my uncle Pinchus that lives in New York. And on the right side is [? Moesa ?] [? Berger. ?] She was shot with a bullet in the back, and she was in the ditch and-- and-- and a lot of shot people. And the German said to the Ukrainian, she's such a beautiful girl, why don't you let her go. Let somebody else kill her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she grabbed from a dead woman a jacket, and she put it on, and she ran away to the forest. And she survived, and she lives now in New York. Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in New York, when I went-- my son graduated from-- from college, from Hebrew Academy, from New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in-- in June of '94.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right side, on the bottom, is my mother, Frida Magid. And on the left side is Bernice, a friend of hers, what she knows for a long time. In the background, on the right side, I am, Harry. And in the middle is Stephanie, that her mother survived, and she lives now in Australia. Sydney, Australia. And on the left of her is my brother, Alex, who lives here in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- maybe about two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in Beverly-- in my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Beverly Hills.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the family of Philip Szerkow's family. On the bottom is the second that's-- that's his daughter. He had that one-- only one daughter. And that's all family. He was-- there was a very big family. They were very, very wealthy. He was the only one that was good to Jews. The rest of them were all anti-Semite. One of the brothers that's in the back, the second from the left, my mother was hiding on his farm. He beat her up. They took off a-- from a-- a piece of wood from the fence, and they-- and they beat her up very badly, and she ran away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Stepan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, must've taken in let's see, maybe five years ago, in 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they send you this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: They send me a picture. I used to send them packages. I used to send her packages. So they send their picture from the family. And Philip Szerkow, he died in Russia. And they sent him out to Siberia. He was a very good man, but the Russian government didn't like him because he was very rich. He had a lot of land. On the left, right by the memorial stone, where they killed the 3,000 Jews from Stepan, is Vera Varshina and her son and her daughter-in-law and her grandchild.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was this memorial?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a memorial made to honor the 3,000 Jews that were killed September 22, 1942. And on the right side is her other son and daughter-in-law and the three kids. And they all leave still in-- no, one just immigrated to Israel. And then the other one is still living in Kostopol with the three kids. And the other one what immigrated to Israel was the one with the wife and the one kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does the memorial say? The

speaker: interviewee
text: Memorial says that here lays 3,000 Jews from Stepan. They were killed by the-- by the Germans and the Ukrainian helpers. And it says, the 22nd of September, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this thing erected? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was erected about four years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the same time. This picture was taken in 1993 by Professor Shimshak from Boston. He went to Stepan to-- he wanted to find out about his family. If anybody survived. And that road was built from the stones from the cemetery was broken up and they build the road from all the stones. In the cemetery, there's isn't even one stone left. Used to be about 1,000 marker stones on our cemetery in Stepan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This place is the [NON-ENGLISH] is about two kilometer from Stepan. They took out 536 Jews, and they shot them here, and they buried them here. And somebody put a chain and there so the cows and the sheeps don't go there to-- to disturb the-- the graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my three children. On the right, Joseph. In the middle, Vera. And on the left is Benjamin. And Joseph is an attorney, and Vera works for a Cartier Jeweler's [INAUDIBLE]. More than eight years. And Joseph is a shomer Shabbos. He don't drive on Shabbos, he don't answer a telephone on Shabbos. And the other ones are not so religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ben?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ben is the student. He goes to Northridge College.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken maybe about two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in the house. In the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Beverly Hills, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: California. This is my picture. I wrote an article in February 1995 in [NON-ENGLISH]. That's a magazine that is published in Israel. And I'm involved in the [NON-ENGLISH] Society. We help a lot of hospitals in Israel. We'll [INAUDIBLE] the crippled soldiers, and also we just sent them an ambulance to Israel for-- [NON-ENGLISH]. The ambulance will arrive next month. And we're doing all charitable work. They don't have no expenses. Everything is volunteered. 100% cause for charity.
